# The prompt lifecycle: where extensions hook in {#sec:prompt-lifecycle}

The lifecycle is the map of extension hook points. Chapters 7-8 assume you can
read this path; this chapter traces the normal interactive lifecycle.
Before reading it, finish [TypeScript used by Pi](#sec:typescript) and
[Architecture](#sec:architecture). The later [Agent core](#sec:agent-core)
chapter explains the reusable loop in more detail; this chapter keeps the
focus on the end-to-end path.

It explains how event-driven transcript updates are requested, not terminal
renderer layout or paint internals; for those, follow the `InteractiveMode`
handlers into the interactive components directory,
`packages/coding-agent/src/modes/interactive/`, and then into
`components/` or `packages/tui`.

It is source-grounded in the interactive mode and session files, plus
`packages/agent/src/agent.ts`, `packages/agent/src/agent-loop.ts`, and the
`packages/ai` dispatch layer.

The exact call graph also has guards for commands, compaction, retries, queued
messages, and other modes. The numbered path summarizes the current source;
its diagrams omit helper calls that do not alter the data/control boundary
being explained.

## A small vocabulary for the trace

An **event** is a record saying that something happened. A **stream** is a
sequence of partial results delivered over time. A **turn** is one model
request and the tool work that follows it. A **settled** run is one with no
retry, compaction, or queued continuation left to perform. A Pi message is
composed of typed **content blocks** such as `text`, `thinking`, and `toolCall`,
rather than one plain string.

Use two prompts to keep the distinction concrete: `"What files changed?"`
usually creates one model turn, while `"Read the config and fix the failing
test"` can create several turns because the model may call `read`, then `bash`,
then answer. The path below explains what stays the same in both examples.

## The complete path

**In plain terms:** your text travels from the editor, through interception and
context construction, to the provider, and back through streaming events, tool
execution, and persistence. The map below is the whole chapter in one picture;
each stage section then zooms in. As a running example, picture the prompt
`"Read the config and fix the failing test"`: it produces several turns because
the model calls `read`, then `bash`, then answers.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-prompt-lifecycle-overview
%%| fig-cap: "The complete interactive prompt path. Stages 1-2 (blue) happen before any model work; stages 6-8 (amber) repeat once per turn; stages 9-10 (grey) settle the run."
flowchart TD
  S1["1-2. Input and interception"]:::input
  S3["3. Turn construction"]:::turn
  S45["4-5. Context and dispatch"]:::turn
  S6["6. Streaming events"]:::turn
  S7["7. Tool execution"]:::turn
  S8{"8. Another turn?"}
  S9["9-10. Persistence<br/>and settlement"]:::settle
  S1 --> S3 --> S45 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8
  S8 -->|"yes"| S45
  S8 -->|"no"| S9
  classDef input fill:#dbe7f5,stroke:#2f5b8f,color:#000000
  classDef turn fill:#fde2c4,stroke:#a05a00,color:#000000
  classDef settle fill:#e9e9e9,stroke:#666666,color:#000000
```

## Stage 1: Terminal input enters the TUI

**In plain terms:** pressing Enter hands your text to the session; pressing it
while the model is still working queues the text for a later turn instead of
interrupting.

`InteractiveMode` owns the coding-agent's interactive screen. Its editor
submission handler recognizes built-in slash commands and `!`/`!!` user-shell
commands before normal agent prompting. A normal idle submission is queued or
resolved through `getUserInput()`. The `run()` loop awaits that method, then
calls `await this.session.prompt(userInput)`.

When Pi is already streaming, regular Enter calls `session.prompt(text,
{ streamingBehavior: "steer" })`; Alt+Enter uses `"followUp"`. **Steering** waits
for the current assistant response and its tool work to finish, then injects
the message before the next provider request. A **follow-up** is held until
there are no more tool calls or steering messages and the run would otherwise
stop, then starts another turn. Neither starts a second concurrent agent run;
each call queues a message at that exact loop boundary. **Compaction**
summarizes older context into a durable entry so the model's context window is
not exceeded; [Sessions and context](#sec:sessions) covers it in detail. During
compaction, non-command input is held by interactive mode until the compaction
flow completes.

The initial prompt supplied by command-line arguments travels through the same
`session.prompt()` API. Print and RPC modes do not instantiate
`InteractiveMode`, but they converge on `AgentSession` rather than
reimplementing the provider path.

**Why an extension author cares:** steering and follow-up are the same queue
modes you select with `sendUserMessage(text, { deliverAs: "steer" | "followUp" })`
— input you inject rides the boundaries described here.

## Stage 2: Commands, keybindings, and input interception

**In plain terms:** before your text becomes a prompt, Pi gives two command
systems and every extension a chance to claim or rewrite it.

There are two distinct command systems:

- Built-in interactive commands such as `/model`, `/settings`, `/new`, and
  `/compact` are handled in `InteractiveMode` before a normal prompt reaches
  the session.
- Extension commands registered with `pi.registerCommand()` are detected first
  inside `AgentSession.prompt()`. They execute immediately, including while an
  agent is streaming, and do not enter the model context unless their handler
  explicitly sends a message.

For ordinary text, `AgentSession.prompt()` asks the extension runner to emit
an `input` event. The event sees raw text after extension-command lookup and
before skill/template expansion. An extension can continue, transform, or
handle it. If it continues, Pi expands `/skill:name ...`; skills are reusable
model instructions and prompt templates are parameterized prompt snippets. Pi
then continues with the expanded text.

**Hook selection comes first:** built-in commands have already been handled by
`InteractiveMode`, and a matching registered extension command has already
returned before `input` is emitted. Therefore `input` is the interception point
for ordinary text and unclaimed slash text. An extension that wants to
intercept a skill invocation must use `input`; one that wants a durable custom
command should use `registerCommand`, not parse slash text manually.

**Why an extension author cares:** this stage decides which interception point
is yours — `registerCommand()` for a new slash command, the `input` event for
transforming or handling ordinary text.

## Stage 3: Agent-session orchestration constructs a turn

**In plain terms:** the session validates that a run can start, gathers your
message plus any buffered extension messages, notifies extensions that a run is
beginning, and hands everything to the generic agent loop.

`AgentMessage` is the agent-core union of provider-compatible LLM `Message`
values and application-defined custom message types. That lets the session
retain rich application messages even when its later conversion step chooses a
different LLM representation.

For a fresh run, `AgentSession.prompt()` validates the selected model and
credentials, checks whether compaction is needed, and creates a user message.
It then appends pending `nextTurn` messages: custom messages buffered by
`sendCustomMessage(..., { deliverAs: "nextTurn" })` (an ExtensionAPI method)
for inclusion with the
*next* fresh user prompt, rather than delivery to an active run. Next it emits
`before_agent_start`, whose handlers can add custom messages and chain a
system-prompt override, then calls `_runAgentPrompt(messages)`.

`_runAgentPrompt()` calls `Agent.prompt()`; the reusable Agent API is detailed
in [Agent core](#sec:agent-core). It can later call `Agent.continue()`
for automatic retry, automatic compaction retry, or work queued by an
`agent_end` handler. On settlement it clears the per-run prompt override,
flushes pending user-bash messages, and emits `agent_settled`.

The `Agent` owns the abort controller, streaming state, accumulated messages,
active tools, and queues. `Agent.prompt()` snapshots its state and starts
`runAgentLoop()`.

**Why an extension author cares:** `before_agent_start` is your last chance to
add messages or override the system prompt before the loop takes over;
`agent_settled` is the signal that all follow-up work is done.

## Stage 4: Context and system prompt cross three construction boundaries

**In plain terms:** Pi builds a system prompt for the model, lets extensions
adjust the message list, and then converts the result into the exact format the
provider API expects.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-context-boundaries
%%| fig-cap: "Stage 4's three construction boundaries. The extension context event (amber) sits between base system-prompt assembly and provider conversion; the output is a Context with exactly systemPrompt, messages, and tools."
flowchart TD
  SP["base system prompt<br/>_rebuildSystemPrompt()"] --> TC["transformContext<br/>extension context event"]:::hook
  TC --> CL["convertToLlm()"]
  CL --> CTX["Context:<br/>systemPrompt + messages + tools"]:::out
  classDef hook fill:#fde2c4,stroke:#a05a00,color:#000000
  classDef out fill:#d7ecd7,stroke:#2e6b2e,color:#000000
```

| Boundary | Owner in the runtime | What it produces |
|----------------|------------------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Base system-prompt assembly | `AgentSession._rebuildSystemPrompt()` and `buildSystemPrompt()` | System-prompt text from cwd, context files, skills, prompts, and active-tool guidance |
| Extension context transform | `Agent`'s `transformContext` callback, wired to the extension `context` event | An adjusted `AgentMessage[]` list before provider conversion |
| LLM/provider conversion | `convertToLlm()` followed by `streamFn`/`streamSimple()` | A provider-compatible `Context`, then the provider's assistant event stream |

These are three transformations for an already-selected model, not three
model-selection steps. The narrative below follows the same order.

Model choice is not part of this stage. The initial model is chosen earlier,
during `createAgentSession()` in `core/sdk.ts` (session restore, configured
default, or `findInitialModel()`), before `new Agent(...)`. The session can
later change `Agent.state.model` (for example through `/model`). When a run
starts, `Agent.prompt()` snapshots the then-current model together with the
system prompt, tools, and transcript. Each assistant request in that run uses
that already-chosen model plus messages accumulated by the loop. The three
boundaries below therefore transform context *for* a chosen model; they do not
select one.

First, the coding agent builds a base system prompt in
`AgentSession._rebuildSystemPrompt()`. That method combines the working
directory, loaded `AGENTS.md`/context files, loaded skills, custom and appended
system prompts, and selected tools plus their snippets and guidelines.
`buildSystemPrompt()` in `core/system-prompt.ts` converts the structured data to
text. Changing active tools rebuilds the base prompt, so tool prompt metadata
can affect the next model turn.

Immediately before *each* model request, `streamAssistantResponse()` in
`agent-loop.ts` crosses the other two boundaries in sequence. It calls the
optional `transformContext` callback on `AgentMessage[]`, then calls
`convertToLlm()` to produce provider-compatible `Message[]`. Coding-agent
supplies both callbacks when creating `Agent` in `core/sdk.ts`:

- `transformContext` dispatches the extension `context` event.
- `convertToLlm` filters and normalizes coding-agent-specific message types.
  When `blockImages` is enabled, this conversion-time step replaces image
  content in converted user and tool-result messages with a text placeholder,
  immediately before provider dispatch. It is dynamic, so a setting change
  affects subsequent conversions.

The resulting `Context` has exactly `systemPrompt`, `messages`, and `tools`.
That shape lets the session persist rich UI-only entries while only a selected
representation reaches the LLM.

When `streamAssistantResponse()` then invokes the installed stream function, it
passes the *already* snapshotted model together with that normalized context.
Provider dispatch (Stage 5) resolves credentials and an adapter for that model;
context construction only prepares the adapter input.

**Why an extension author cares:** the `context` event is where you reshape the
message list the model is about to see — per request, not just at run start.

## Stage 5: Provider dispatch for the already-chosen model

**In plain terms:** the loop hands the normalized context and the chosen model
to a small dispatch function, which adds credentials and headers and picks the
provider adapter that speaks the vendor's wire protocol.

This stage charts the coding-agent stream path from the snapshotted `Model` to a
provider adapter. Model *catalog* selection and runtime changes belong to
session creation and `/model` (see Stage 4's opening and
`developer-guide/11-models-providers-and-auth.qmd`); they are not re-decided
here.

Coding-agent installs a custom `streamFn` on `Agent` in `core/sdk.ts`. For each
assistant request the path is:

1. `streamAssistantResponse()` (agent-loop) calls `streamFn(model, context,
   options)` with the snapshotted model and the Stage 4 `Context`.
2. That `streamFn` resolves credentials with
   `modelRegistry.getApiKeyAndHeaders(model)`, merges provider-attribution and
   request headers (`mergeProviderAttributionHeaders`), then emits extension
   `before_provider_headers` so handlers can mutate the assembled headers.
3. `streamFn` calls `streamSimple(model, context, options)` from
   `packages/ai/src/compat.ts`, passing `apiKey`, merged headers, timeouts, and
   retries. Agent core also forwards `onPayload` / `onResponse` from the
   `Agent` instance into those options (wired in `createLoopConfig()`).
4. `compat.streamSimple` either delegates to built-in model support
   (`compatModels.streamSimple`) when the model matches a registered builtin
   API instance, or looks up the registered API provider by `model.api` and
   calls that provider's `streamSimple`.
5. The adapter serializes the normalized context and returns a
   provider-independent assistant event stream. During that path:
   - `onPayload` becomes extension `before_provider_request` and may replace
     the provider-specific payload after serialization.
   - `onResponse` becomes extension `after_provider_response` and observes HTTP
     status/headers before body consumption when the adapter exposes them.

`ModelRegistry` still owns the catalog, configured provider overrides,
credential lookup, OAuth state, and header sources used by step 2; its role in
this stage is auth/header resolution for the chosen model, not model selection.

The sequence below summarizes stages 3-6 for one turn of the running example:

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-turn-sequence
%%| fig-cap: "One turn across stages 3-6: turn construction, context conversion, provider dispatch, and the event stream back to the session. The turn begins when the editor calls AgentSession.prompt(text)."
sequenceDiagram
  participant S as AgentSession
  participant X as Extensions
  participant L as Agent loop
  S->>X: before_agent_start
  S->>L: prompt(messages)
  L->>X: context event, convertToLlm
  L->>L: streamSimple to provider
  L-->>S: message events
  L->>X: tool_call gate
  L->>L: execute tools
  L-->>S: toolResult
```

Scope boundary for this chapter: the numbered path stops at adapter
`streamSimple` and the three provider-extension insertion points. Vendor wire
formats, transport negotiation, custom provider registration, and auth-storage
details live in `developer-guide/11-models-providers-and-auth.qmd` and the
provider-hook reference in
`packages/coding-agent/docs/extensions.md` (`before_provider_headers`,
`before_provider_request`, `after_provider_response`). Those headers/payload
hooks are not substitutes for a custom provider; use `pi.registerProvider()`
when you must declare a model catalog, endpoint, auth, or streaming
implementation.

**Why an extension author cares:** the header/payload hooks
(`before_provider_headers`, `before_provider_request`,
`after_provider_response`) fire inside this stage, in the order shown in the
turn diagram above.

## Stage 6: Streaming response becomes agent events and TUI updates

**In plain terms:** the provider's partial replies are consumed chunk by chunk
and republished as Pi's own events; the transcript you see typing out is those
events being rendered.

`streamAssistantResponse()` consumes the `AssistantMessageEventStream` with
`for await`: this is the async-iteration boundary where provider deltas become
agent events. The [Agent core](#sec:agent-core) chapter shows the same loop
from the reusable runtime's point of view. It creates a partial assistant
message on `start`, replaces that partial message as text/thinking/tool-call
deltas arrive, and emits `message_start`, `message_update`, and `message_end`
events.

`Agent.processEvents()` reduces those events into state and then awaits all
subscribers. `AgentSession` bridges generic events to persistence, extensions,
and application behavior. `InteractiveMode` updates the visible transcript,
current streaming row, tool rows, footer, and render requests. The TUI package
performs the actual terminal rendering.

The extension lifecycle maps directly onto this path: `agent_start`,
`turn_start`, `message_start`, many `message_update` events, `message_end`,
then later `turn_end` and `agent_end`.

For canonical detail, use [Agent core](#sec:agent-core) for loop state and
queue semantics, [Built-in tools](#sec:tools) for tool contracts,
[Extension event reference](#sec:events) for event payloads, and [Failure and
recovery](#sec:failure) for recovery behavior. This chapter keeps only the
end-to-end trace.

**Why an extension author cares:** `message_start`/`message_update`/
`message_end` and the turn events are how you observe model output live — for
example to stream progress into custom UI.

## Stage 7: Tool calls are parsed, validated, gated, and executed

**In plain terms:** when the model asks to run tools, Pi checks each request
against the tool's schema, lets extensions veto or modify it, executes it, and
appends the result where the model can see it.

After a final assistant message, `agent-loop.ts` filters content blocks whose
`type` is `"toolCall"`. A `"length"` stop means the token limit cut off the
assistant output, so any tool call may contain truncated JSON arguments even if
its parsed block appears complete. The loop therefore fails every tool call in
that message rather than risk side effects from incomplete arguments.

For each normal tool call, the loop's short pipeline is:

1. Emit `tool_execution_start`, look up the active tool, and prepare and
   validate its arguments against the TypeBox schema.
2. Run the `beforeToolCall` gate (coding-agent's `tool_call` event), then call
   `tool.execute(id, args, signal, onUpdate)`.
3. Turn `onUpdate` values into update events, apply the `afterToolCall`
   (`tool_result`) patch, emit `tool_execution_end`, and append a `toolResult`
   message.

See [Built-in tools](#sec:tools) for the contract and [Extension event
reference](#sec:events) for payloads and hook ordering.

Agent core defaults `toolExecution` to `"parallel"`
(`options.toolExecution ?? "parallel"` in `packages/agent/src/agent.ts`; see
[Agent core](#sec:agent-core) for the ordering contract).
Coding-agent's `new Agent({...})` in `core/sdk.ts` does not pass
`toolExecution`, so that default remains. A caller of agent core can select
sequential execution, and a tool declaring `executionMode: "sequential"` makes
its containing batch sequential. Otherwise, preflight happens in assistant
source order, successful tool executions may run concurrently, and final
`toolResult` messages are emitted in assistant source order. Start/update/end
event ordering differs in parallel mode; see the extension documentation
before using sibling tool results as state.

Built-in tools are supplied by coding-agent. They are ordinary tools at this
boundary, which is why an extension can add a tool or override a built-in one.
Custom filesystem-mutating tools must participate in
`withFileMutationQueue()` to avoid lost writes when sibling tools run in
parallel; the [Built-in tools](#sec:tools) chapter has the runnable queue
skeleton.

**Why an extension author cares:** the `tool_call` event is your safety gate
(veto or rewrite arguments before execution) and `tool_result` lets you patch
what the model sees afterwards.

## Stage 8: Results create the next agent action

**In plain terms:** tool results go back to the model; final text ends the turn
unless someone — you, via steering or follow-up — has queued more to say.

Each final tool result is appended to the in-memory agent context and becomes
the model-facing material for the next turn. If at least one tool call was
executed and the batch was not explicitly terminating, the loop starts another
assistant response. If the model instead returns ordinary final text, the loop
checks steering messages first and follow-up messages when it would otherwise
stop:

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-next-action
%%| fig-cap: "After a toolResult is appended, the loop decides what happens next. Queued steering beats follow-up; an empty loop emits agent_end."
flowchart TD
  Q1{"tool calls and<br/>not terminating?"}
  Q1 -->|"yes"| NT["next model turn"]:::cont
  Q1 -->|"no"| Q2{"steering queued?"}
  Q2 -->|"yes"| NT
  Q2 -->|"no"| Q3{"follow-up queued?"}
  Q3 -->|"yes"| NT
  Q3 -->|"no"| END["agent_end"]:::term
  classDef cont fill:#d7ecd7,stroke:#2e6b2e,color:#000000
  classDef term fill:#e9e9e9,stroke:#666666,color:#000000
```

A tool can return `terminate: true` to hint that the next model call should be
skipped, but the batch terminates only when every finalized tool result sets
that flag. A tool signals failure by throwing; merely returning a field named
`isError` does not mark it failed.

**Why an extension author cares:** messages you queued with
`deliverAs: "steer"` or `"followUp"` are drained here, in that priority order.

## Stage 9: Persistence and transcript updates settle the turn

**In plain terms:** everything that happened is appended to the durable session
file and, in parallel, to the on-screen transcript; custom UI renderers only
change the screen, never the model loop.

As message events complete, `AgentSession` records them through its
`SessionManager`. The session is a JSONL tree, so the persisted form can later
rebuild a branch and compact context. Model and thinking-level changes are also
session entries. Extension state added with `appendEntry()` is persisted as a
custom entry but remains outside LLM context.

`InteractiveMode.handleEvent()` responds to the same event stream. Assistant
updates request transcript updates for the streaming message; tool lifecycle
events manage pending tool rows; finalized messages become permanent transcript
components. Renderer registration lets an extension replace the display of its
custom messages, entries, or tool calls/results without changing how the model
loop works. This chapter stops at that event-to-component boundary; the title
intentionally does not promise terminal rendering internals.

**Why an extension author cares:** `appendEntry()` persists your state without
polluting model context, and renderer registration controls how your custom
messages and entries look on screen.

## Stage 10: Errors, cancellation, retries, compaction, and shutdown

**In plain terms:** a run can fail, be cancelled, retry, compact, and finally
settle; `agent_end` only means the loop stopped, while `agent_settled` means
nothing automatic is left to do.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-run-lifecycle
%%| fig-cap: "Run lifecycle. Retries and compaction resume the run; agent_end fires when the loop stops, agent_settled when no automatic continuation remains."
stateDiagram-v2
  running: running
  retrying: retrying
  compacting: compacting
  ended: agent_end<br/>loop stopped
  settled: agent_settled<br/>no retry or queue left
  aborted: aborted
  running --> retrying: provider error
  running --> compacting: context too large
  retrying --> running
  compacting --> running
  running --> ended
  ended --> settled
  running --> aborted: Esc / abort signal
```

The low-level loop deliberately separates provider failures from tool
failures: a provider error or abort ends the run with an assistant message,
whereas a schema error, blocked call, or thrown tool execution becomes an
error `toolResult` that the model can recover from. Esc aborts the active
`AbortController`; provider, tool, and extension work must honor the signal.
For the complete recovery matrix, see [Failure and recovery](#sec:failure).

Above this boundary, `AgentSession` may retry, compact older context, rebuild
it, and continue; [Sessions and context](#sec:sessions) explains compaction.
Session replacement or process exit emits `session_shutdown`, where
session-bound resources should be cleaned up. `agent_end` means the current
low-level loop stopped. `agent_settled` is later: Pi has no automatic retry,
compaction retry, or queued continuation left. Status integrations and cleanup
that require true idleness should use `agent_settled` or `ctx.waitForIdle()` in
a command context.

**Why an extension author cares:** honor the abort signal in your own async
work, clean up session-bound resources on `session_shutdown`, and use
`agent_settled` (not `agent_end`) when you need true idleness.

## Trace this yourself

All source paths in this chapter are relative to the repository root. For a
live trace, start at these symbols in order:

1. `InteractiveMode.run()` and `getUserInput()`.
2. `AgentSession.prompt()` and `_runAgentPrompt()`.
3. `Agent.prompt()`, `createLoopConfig()`, and `processEvents()`.
4. `runAgentLoop()`, `streamAssistantResponse()`, and `executeToolCalls()`.
5. `streamSimple()` in `packages/ai/src/compat.ts`.
6. The `streamSimple` function in the selected provider API adapter.

Use the extension event timeline in `packages/coding-agent/docs/extensions.md`
as a second view of the same lifecycle. It documents what extension authors
can rely on; the source symbols above show which layer makes each guarantee.

## Stage-to-surface bridge

| Stage | Hook or API available there |
|---|---|
| Input | `sendUserMessage`, `deliverAs: "steer"` or `"followUp"` |
| Interception | `registerCommand`, `input` event |
| Turn construction | `before_agent_start`, `sendMessage`, `triggerTurn` |
| Context boundaries | `context` event, `getSystemPromptOptions()` |
| Provider dispatch | `before_provider_headers`, `before_provider_request`, `after_provider_response`, `registerProvider` |
| Streaming | `message_start`/`update`/`end`, `turn_start`/`end`, custom UI |
| Tools | `tool_call`, `tool_result`, `registerTool` |
| Next action | steering and follow-up queues |
| Persistence | `appendEntry`, message and entry renderers |
| Settlement | `agent_end`, `agent_settled`, `session_shutdown`, `waitForIdle` |

This table is the bridge into the extension recipes. Use the [extension event
reference](#sec:events) for exact payloads and return values.
